headlines: BlackRock IBIT sees third consecutive week of net inflows exceeding $300M
BTC dominance holds above 54% as altcoin season narrative fades
Realized price now below spot — average holder in profit, historically a continuation signal

news: Saylor posts 'The Orange March Continues' on X following IBIT flow data
STRF yield spread over comparable bonds widest in 6 weeks — risk premium elevated
No new capital raise announced; last raise closed March 18

institutional.blackrock: IBIT AUM crosses $52B. No new ETH ETF news. Larry Fink reiterates 'tokenization of all assets' thesis at Paris conference.
institutional.fidelity: FBTC steady inflows. Fidelity Digital Assets publishes institutional custody expansion roadmap for 2026.
institutional.regulatoryRadar: Senate Banking Committee schedules crypto market structure hearing for April 8. Morgan Stanley Bitcoin ETF S-1 amendment filed March 18 — still under SEC review.
institutional.sovereign: No new nation-state BTC news today.

creatorIntel.sentimentNote: Creators broadly constructive on price structure but flagging macro uncertainty — war premium and stagflation concerns tempering euphoria.

videos.title: Bitcoin's Next Move: Why $90K Resistance Matters More Than You Think
videos.creator: Preston Pysh — Bitcoin Fundamentals
videos.summary: Pysh argues the $90K level is psychologically and technically significant — three prior rejection wicks at this level. He frames the current accumulation phase as similar to mid-2024 pre-halving behavior. Key thesis: LTH supply absorption is the dominant signal, not price action. Discusses how ETF inflows are masking underlying exchange outflows.
videos.whyItMatters: Pysh's on-chain framing aligns with today's LTH accumulation data — adds conviction to the outflow signal.

videos.title: Lyn Alden: The Macro Case for Bitcoin in a Stagflation Scenario
videos.creator: Lyn Alden Investment Strategy
videos.summary: Alden makes the case that stagflation — rising prices with slowing growth — historically favors hard assets. She positions BTC as a 'monetary energy' store in this environment, distinct from equities. Cautions that the correlation to risk assets could spike in a credit event, which remains the primary tail risk. Recommends dollar-cost averaging over lump-sum entry at current levels.
videos.whyItMatters: Direct relevance to current macro backdrop — war in Mideast + Fed uncertainty = the exact stagflation setup Alden describes.

videos.title: Saylor's Strategy Instruments: Which One Should You Own?
videos.creator: Matthew Kratter — Bitcoin University
videos.summary: Kratter breaks down MSTR, STRD, STRK, and STRF for retail investors. Argues STRF offers the most risk-adjusted yield at current prices — 10%+ with BTC exposure optionality. Notes that preferred instruments trade at a yield premium vs. comparable corporate bonds, which he frames as 'getting paid to hold crypto-adjacent paper.' Warns NAV premium on MSTR common is historically elevated.
videos.whyItMatters: Retail-focused breakdown of Strategy instrument stack — relevant given today's STRF yield spread data.

videos.title: Bitcoin Fear & Greed at 62 — What History Says Happens Next
videos.creator: Willy Woo
videos.summary: Woo analyzes historical F&G readings at the 60-65 range and their forward returns. Finds median 60-day return of +18% from this zone when on-chain metrics are constructive. Current read: on-chain is constructive, macro is mixed. Key risk flag: DXY strength could cap BTC upside if the correlation to risk-off assets holds.
videos.whyItMatters: Quantitative historical framing — helps calibrate expectations from current F&G 62 reading.

videos.title: Dan Held: Why This Bitcoin Cycle Is Different
videos.creator: Dan Held
videos.summary: Held argues institutional infrastructure (ETFs, custody, options market) has fundamentally changed BTC's supply/demand dynamics. The 'sell the halving' playbook is obsolete when ETF inflows can absorb mined supply multiples over. Remains constructive on 6-12 month price action. Notes retail is still largely absent — typically a mid-cycle signal.
videos.whyItMatters: Structural argument that ETF demand has changed the halving cycle — relevant context for current flow data.

weeklyMoves.headline: OpenAI launches GPT-5 API access for enterprise customers
weeklyMoves.whyItMatters: Enterprise API access before consumer launch is a deliberate moat-building move — locks in developer tooling and workflow dependencies before competitors respond.

weeklyMoves.headline: Anthropic raises $3.5B Series E at $61B valuation
weeklyMoves.whyItMatters: War chest enables compute scaling and enterprise sales buildout — narrows the funding gap with OpenAI significantly.

weeklyMoves.headline: NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra samples ship to hyperscalers
weeklyMoves.whyItMatters: Blackwell Ultra extends NVIDIA's compute lead by 12-18 months — AMD and Intel response not shipping until late 2026 at earliest.

weeklyMoves.headline: Meta releases Llama 4 'Scout' open weights — 109B parameters
weeklyMoves.whyItMatters: Llama 4 Scout benchmarks competitively with GPT-4o on coding tasks — keeps open-source viable and pressures closed-model pricing.

aiRace.deepDiveSummary: NVIDIA's moat is CUDA — 15 years of developer tooling, libraries, and institutional knowledge that cannot be replicated by switching to ROCm or any new framework. The real lock-in isn't hardware; it's the software ecosystem. AMD's MI300X competes on raw FLOPs but trails on inference efficiency and software maturity. TSMC dependency is NVIDIA's primary systemic risk — a Taiwan strait event would halt Blackwell production entirely with no fallback. Until CoWoS advanced packaging capacity expands, supply constraints will cap revenue upside regardless of demand.

snapshot.openai: Dominant API market share (~60% enterprise). GPT-5 launch expands lead. O3 reasoning model sets new benchmark ceiling.
snapshot.anthropic: Claude strongest on safety and long-context. Enterprise sales accelerating. Constitutional AI is a regulatory differentiator.
snapshot.google: Gemini 2.0 competitive on multimodal. TPU vertical integration is the most capital-efficient compute play. Still trailing on developer mindshare.
snapshot.meta: Llama open weights strategy is a bet that commoditizing models benefits Meta's ad business. FAIR research output remains world-class.
snapshot.xai: Grok 3 real-time data moat from X platform is unique — no competitor has this. Compute ambitions (100K GPU cluster) credible.
snapshot.nvidia: CUDA moat + Blackwell supply constraints = pricing power through 2027. NIM inference platform expanding enterprise stickiness.
snapshot.tsmc: Single point of failure for the entire AI supply chain. CoWoS capacity remains the binding constraint on AI chip output globally.

investorBehavior: Post-2022 recovery caused portfolio drift — many 60/40 portfolios now sitting at 70-80% equities; advisors actively rebalancing back to targets
'Safety feels expensive in 2026' — yield-chasing via JEPI/JEPQ/QYLD introducing unintended equity correlation that investors don't fully appreciate
Advisors counseling against full cash: 'The bigger risk isn't a bad quarter — it's running out of money in year 22'
Three-bucket framework gaining traction: 1-2yr cash, 3-7yr income layer, long-tail growth bucket
Sequence-of-returns risk is the central near-retiree debate; current war/stagflation environment intensifying the conversation
planningPoints: Social Security: delay strategies still mathematically optimal for most — 8% per year delayed past 62 until 70. Current discussion: with SS trust fund depletion projected ~2033, healthy near-retirees should model both claiming strategies.
QLACs: $210K limit (2025-2026 per SECURE 2.0), excluded from RMDs — worth modeling for longevity insurance if single-premium budget allows.
SECURE Act 2.0: RMD age now 73; Roth IRA no longer subject to RMDs during owner's lifetime (effective 2024) — significant planning opportunity for those with large traditional IRA balances.

Municipal Bonds: Bloomberg Muni Index YTW ~3.6%. 30-year IG muni 4.09%; tax-equivalent at 40.8% effective rate = 6.5-6.9%. CA/NY in-state investors: 2.77-3.06% muni ≈ 5-6% taxable equivalent. $600B supply expected 2026 — reinvestment demand strong.
Annuities: Fixed deferred credited rates 4.5-5.5%. SPIAs (65yo, $100K) ~6.5-7.5% annual payout. 'Floor plus upside' framework dominant among advisors. Demand elevated — lock in before rates move.
retirement.actionItem: With 10-year yield at 4.21% and muni tax-equivalent yields at 6.5-6.9% for high earners, this week's action: review your fixed income allocation. If you're in taxable accounts and in the 32%+ bracket, a 30-year IG muni ladder beats T-notes after-tax by 200-250bps. $600B supply in 2026 means selection, not scarcity.

heartSignal.finding: JAMA Cardiology (March 2026): Adults 60+ who maintained resting heart rate below 65 bpm through aerobic exercise showed 34% lower risk of major adverse cardiovascular events over 10 years vs. sedentary controls. Zone 2 cardio (3-4x/week, 45 min) was the minimum effective dose in the cohort.

supplements.name: Magnesium Glycinate
supplements.evidence: Strong evidence for sleep quality improvement in adults 60+. Emerging evidence for blood pressure reduction (systolic -3-5 mmHg in deficient populations). Glycinate form has superior GI tolerance vs. oxide or citrate. Deficiency common in older adults due to reduced absorption.

supplements.name: Omega-3 / EPA+DHA
supplements.evidence: Multiple RCTs confirm cardiovascular benefit at 4g/day (REDUCE-IT trial: 25% reduction in major cardiovascular events with icosapentaenoic acid). VITAL trial showed modest benefit at lower doses. Anti-inflammatory effects well-established. 2026 AHA guidelines: reasonable for those with elevated triglycerides or established CVD.

supplements.name: NMN (Nicotinamide Mononucleotide)
supplements.evidence: NAD+ precursor — human trials show NMN raises blood NAD+ levels (replicated). Whether higher NAD+ translates to meaningful longevity or functional benefit in humans remains unproven. Animal data compelling; human RCTs small and short. David Sinclair's self-experimentation widely cited but not controlled evidence.

redLight.protocol: 660nm (red) + 850nm (near-infrared). 10-20 min daily, 6-12 inches from panel. Whole-body or targeted (chest/back for cardiovascular; joints for inflammation). Morning preferred — circadian alignment with natural light exposure.
redLight.researchNote: Photobiomodulation Therapy journal (Feb 2026): 12-week PBM protocol (660/850nm, 20 min/day) in adults 65+ showed significant reduction in inflammatory markers (CRP, IL-6) and improved grip strength vs. sham. Sample size small (n=48) but results consistent with prior mechanistic literature.

sauna.protocol: Finnish sauna: 180-190°F (82-88°C), 15-20 min sessions, 3-4x per week. Cold plunge or cool shower optional but not required for cardiovascular benefit. Avoid within 2 hours of sleep — core temperature elevation can delay sleep onset.
sauna.finding: Rhonda Patrick synthesis (March 2026): Updated analysis of Kuopio Ischemic Heart Disease cohort (2,315 Finnish men) confirms dose-response relationship — 4+ sauna sessions/week associated with 50% lower cardiovascular mortality vs. 1x/week. Effect persists after controlling for exercise, smoking, and BMI. Mechanism: repeated heat stress mimics aerobic exercise for vascular adaptation.

studyOfWeek.title: Creatine Supplementation and Cognitive Function in Adults Over 60: A Randomized Controlled Trial
studyOfWeek.finding: Adults 60+ taking 5g creatine monohydrate daily for 24 weeks showed significant improvement on executive function and memory tests vs. placebo (p<0.01). Effect size modest but consistent — particularly pronounced in participants with lower baseline dietary creatine intake (vegetarians/vegans).
studyOfWeek.relevance: Creatine is one of the most studied, safest, and cheapest supplements available — this trial adds cognitive benefit to the established physical performance evidence for 60+ adults.

health.practitionerTake: This week's data supports a simple, evidence-grounded stack for cardiovascular and longevity optimization: Zone 2 cardio 3-4x/week (the single highest-ROI intervention), Omega-3 at therapeutic dose (4g EPA+DHA if elevated TG or CVD risk), Magnesium Glycinate for sleep and BP, and Creatine for muscle and now cognitive preservation. Sauna 3-4x/week if accessible — the cardiovascular data is now strong enough that a longevity physician would recommend it as adjunct to exercise, not alternative. Red light therapy is promising but evidence base is thin enough that it's 'reasonable to try, not required.' NMN: interesting mechanistically, but spend money on the above four first.
